Snow amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches and the following roads were closed due to snow, blowing snow and drifting:  US Highway 20 from Ashton to the Montana state line, Idaho Highway 32 from Ashton to Tetonia, and Idaho Highway 33 from Newdale to Tetonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another powerful and moist Pacific storm brought heavy snow and strong winds yet again to southeast Idaho.  Multiple roads were closed again due to snow and wind with heavy amounts recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another round of snow and extreme wind gusts over 40 mph caused extensive road closures.  Residents of Roberts were advised to shelter in place due to the extreme road conditions and Roberts Elementary School was closed on the 27th.   Jefferson School District 251 Roberts residents were also excused from attending.   Snow amounts were 4 to 6 inches with 4.5 inches in Idaho Falls.  The following road closures occurred:  US Highway 20 from Idaho Falls to the US Highway intersection, US Highway 26 from Ririe to the Wyoming state line,  and Idaho Highway 33 from Newdale to Tetonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another powerful and moist Pacific storm brought heavy snow and strong winds yet again to southeast Idaho.  Multiple roads were closed again due to snow and wind with heavy amounts recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured snow amounts were 8 inches at Hailey, 4.5 inches at Bellevue, and 4 inches at Picabo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another Pacific storm system brought another year of strong winds, heavy snow with road closures, hazardous travel and school closings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated SNOTEL snowfall amounts were 13 inches at Bostetter Ranger Station and 24 inches at Howell Canyon. Generally lesser amounts, less than 8 inches, fell across lower elevations during this long duration winter event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March began with heavy snowfall over an extended period with some areas receiving over a foot mainly in the Upper Snake River Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall amounts were 11 inches at the Island Park COOP, 12 inches in Macks Inn, and 13 inches at Harriman State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold temperatures combined with high winds to cause dangerously cold wind chills from the 29th through the 31st.   Several schools closed on the 30th and 31st due to the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty to 30 below zero temperatures were widespread on the 30th with wind chills 50 below zero or colder.  The Mackay School District 182 was closed on the 30th due to the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold temperatures combined with high winds to cause dangerously cold wind chills from the 29th through the 31st.   Several schools closed on the 30th and 31st due to the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minus 15 to -30 temperatures were widespread on the 30th with the coldest -28 at Taber with -23 at Blackfoot.   -25 to -40 wind chills were common.   The following schools were closed due to the extreme cold on the 30th and 31st:  Pocatello-Chubbuck School District 25, American Falls School District 381, Snake River School District 52, Blackfoot School District 55, Aberdeen School District 58, Shelley School District 60, Firth School District 59, and Shoshone Bannock School District 537.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold temperatures combined with high winds to cause dangerously cold wind chills from the 29th through the 31st.   Several schools closed on the 30th and 31st due to the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minus 28 degrees was recorded at Idahome on the 30th.   Wind chills reached 40 below or colder.   Due to the extreme cold Rockland School District 382 was closed on the 30th and Oneida School District 351 started 2 hours late.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold temperatures combined with high winds to cause dangerously cold wind chills from the 29th through the 31st.   Several schools closed on the 30th and 31st due to the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A temperatures of 31 below zero was measured at the Driggs airport and -26 in Ashton on the 30th.   Wind chill readings were widespread below 50 below zero.   Fremont County School District 215 and Teton School District 401 were both closed on the 30th and 31st due to the extreme cold conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell mainly in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 20 inches of snow fell at the Oxford Springs SNOTEL site. The heaviest snow was confined to the higher peaks. Lesser amounts, generally under 8 inches, fell in the lower elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell mainly in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated SNOTEL snow amounts were 28 inches at Sedgwick Peak and 11 inches at Wildhorse Divide.  The Inkom COOP site measured 8 inches of snow. Lesser amounts, generally under 8 inches, fell in the lower elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continual influx of Pacific moisture and colder than normal temperatures brought rounds of very heavy snow, strong winds, avalanches, and road closures throughout southeast Idaho in an extended period.   Events were difficult to separate and treated as one continual event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts during this multi-day event from the 24th through the 26th were 10.4 inches at the Island Park COOP site, 12 inches at Macks Inn, and the White Elephant SNOTEL had an estimated 14 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 16.5 inches of snow fell at the Vienna Mine SNOTEL site over this multi-day snow event. Lesser amounts, generally under 8 inches, fell in lower elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough moved into the Intermountain West, bringing widespread precipitation during the night of January 18th. A cold front passed during the night, changing rain to snow in the Boise metropolitan area which created especially icy and slippery roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WFO measured 2.9 of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough moved into the Intermountain West, bringing widespread precipitation during the night of January 18th. A cold front passed during the night, changing rain to snow in the Boise metropolitan area which created especially icy and slippery roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ada County Sheriff attributed 48 total traffic accidents to the snowfall and resulting slippery road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winter temperatures were the in the top 5 for coldest resulting in significant ice buildup on the Lemhi River. Cold temperatures culminated on January 30th with a low of -33F in Leadore and -18F in Salmon. Ice jam related flooding occurred around this significant cold period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jam related flooding damaged 3 homes and properties and flooded driveways of 10-12 additional homes. Hay fields were covered with 3 feet of ice with damage to stored hay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across southwest Idaho early Tuesday evening and produced |heavy rain and strong winds across portions of the Lower Treasure Valley, including the city of Boise. Social media, weather spotters, and emergency manager reports indicated flooding of many streets in downtown Boise and the North-End Boise area. Water overwhelmed  storm water drainages and flowed onto sidewalks and lawns. Water up to two feet deep left several vehicles stranded in the downtown area. The Connector, a major commuter route to downtown Boise was flooded, as were some apartments, and several buildings on the Boise State University campus. Rainfall rates over one inch an hour occurred with localized  rainfall totals up to 1.85 inches between 6 PM and 10 PM MDT. ||Blowing dust reducing visibility and high winds formed with a strong outflow boundary near the Nevada border around 5pm MDT. Wind damage was reported from Grandview, ID to downtown Meridian, ID.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station, GDVI, 5.2 miles southeast of Grandview, ID measured a wind gust of 54 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system paired with a mid-level shortwave trough produced bands of heavy snow and strong wind in the Treasure Valley and Snake River Plain. Several transportation impacts occurred, with multiple accidents along Interstate 84 near Mountain Home reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 20, a warm front pushed through the region bringing strong winds across portions of the Idaho Panhandle. On February 21, an Arctic cold front moved through the area bringing another round of strong winds. It also brought brief heavy snow that reduced visibility and created hazardous travel conditions to North Idaho Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several snow squalls impacted the area bringing strong winds, low visibility, and slick roads from Sandpoint through Coeur D'Alene. Traffic cameras captured the squall affects. Snow amounts were less than inch. Windspeeds ranged from 25 to 40 MPH. ASOS at Sandpoint Airport report visibility of a quarter mile.

Highway 47 was closed from Ashton to Bear Gulch.  North Fremont High School and Ashton Elementary School also closed on the 21st and 22nd. The Burley Municipal Airport had gusts of 66 and 68 mph 4 hours apart at around 0730 adn 1130 MDT.   Rupert measured 75 mph.  Two semi tractor trailers blown over at different locations on interstate 86 between exit 15 and 33.  A metal shid blown off foundation and down the block in Rupert.  Another smi tractor trailer blown over in the median along interstate 84 near Burley.  Widespread power outages were reported as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow combined with wind gusts up to 40 mph and higher closed roads and schools on the 14th and 15th due to hazardous road conditions.  Th following schools were closed on the 14th:  Bonneville Joint District 93, Fremont District 215, Jefferson District 251, Ririe District 252, Madison District 321.  Interstate 15 was closed from Idaho Falls to the Montana border due to the snow and wind by the evening of the 14th.  Snowfall amounts were 4 inches Hamer, 7 inches Rexburg, 5 inches Idaho Falls, and 5 inches Rigby.

Wind gusts of 35 to 60 mph were common throughout the day on April 1st and the highways closed between noon and 5 PM MDT and remained close until the 2nd.   Spotter and COOP observers measured in the range of 8 to 10 inches of snowfall in Island Park.

Drifting snow was extensive with visibilities lowered to zero.   Roads closed between noon and 5 pm MDT on the 1st and re-opened on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable pattern set up for extremely heavy snow in the Pocatello area where amounts of snow were generally in the 1 to 2 foot range throughout the metro area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to 8 inches of snow was measured in the Inkom area with snow increasing rapidly to the north and west with a high measured total of 25.5 inches 7 miles northwest of Inkom off of Buckskin Road.    Arbon Elementary School District 383 closed schools on the 4th as well as Rockland School District 382 due to the snow and wind causing hazardous travel.

The following schools were closed on the 14th and 15th due to road conditions:  Pocatello/Chubbuck District 25, Snake River District 52, Blackfoot District 55, Aberdeen District 58, Firth District 59, Shelley District 60, and Shoshone Bannock District 537.  Some snow amounts were 5 inches at Pocatello Airport, 7 inches in town Pocatello and Chubbuck, 10 inches Pocatello benches, 7.5 inches Blackfoot, 6.5 inches Fort Hall, and 8 inches Aberdeen.  A major accident on interstate 15 near Pocatello closed interstate 15 southbound for nearly 3 hours at 4 pm on the 14th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low from the Pacific Northwest moved in and brought terrain enhanced snow accumulations to higher elevations in the West Central Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 24 hour snow totals in the West Central Mountains are as follows: 15.0 inches near the Tamarack Ski Resort, 7.0 inches at the McCall town snow stake, and 12.0 inches near the Brundage Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable pattern set up for extremely heavy snow in the Pocatello area where amounts of snow were generally in the 1 to 2 foot range throughout the metro area.   Schools were closed and morning rush hour travel on the 4th was extremely difficult as blowing and drifting snow was also widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A favorable localized heavy snow pattern set up with extremely heavy snowfall from American Falls eastward to Pocatello and surrounding areas. Snow amounts escalated from 5.5 inches in Aberdeen to 5 inches in American Falls and 9 inches by the American Falls COOP observer. The Pocatello Regional Airport measured 11.7 inches with downtown Pocatello 12.5 inches. Areas around Chubbuck measured from 15 to 21 inches with the Pocatello benches also measuring 15 to 21 inches. A public report 4 miles east/southeast of Chubbuck carried 25 inches which was the highest total reported. Winds of 15 to 25 mph caused extensive blowing and drifting with horrible morning travel on the 4th. Pocatello Chubbuck School District 25 closed as did American Falls School District 381. Sho-Ban School District closed and Idaho State University closed afternoon classes as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought heavy snow and high winds and extensive travel difficulties on the 10th.   US Highway 26 closed between Arco and Carey.  Wind gusts over 50 mph were widespread with a 62 mph gust measured at Dead Man Canyon and over 50 mph measured at Howe, Arco and Berenice.  Snowfall totals were measures at 9 inches at Moore and 8 inches at Arco and Mackay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late winter Pacific storm brought heavy snow, heavy rain and strong winds which closed roads across southeastern Idaho.  Reports of flooding and wind damage also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph were widespread ahead of a cold front.  The maximum was 76 mph at the Pocatello Airport ASOS at 856 AM MDT.   Other gusts were 71 mph at American Falls, 69 mph at Fort Hall, 62 mph in Chubbuck, and 58 mph in Aberdeen.   Nearly 2,000 customers suffered power outages.   Sheet metal blew off roof of Master Mechanix in Pocatello.  Lrge evergreen tree 2 foot in diameter uprooted in American Falls, Street light pole blown over on Pocatello Creek Rd in Pocatello, Tree limbs down, shids blown over, shingles blown off on northwest side of Chubbuck,l Lagre highway sign downed at interstate 86 Pocatello Airport exit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late winter Pacific storm brought heavy snow, heavy rain and strong winds which closed roads across southeastern Idaho.  Reports of flooding and wind damage also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front brought strong winds and heavy snow to the region.   US Highway was closed from Carey to Arco on the 10th due to the snow and wind.   The Richfield COOP site recorded 6 inches of snowfall.   Wind gusts of 51 mph were measured at Potter Butte and 11 miles north of Shoshone.  Richfield had a 49 mph wind gust.   The wind gusts caused power outages and 2,500 customers were without power in the Shoshone and Kimama areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late winter Pacific storm brought heavy snow, heavy rain and strong winds which closed roads across southeastern Idaho.  Reports of flooding and wind damage also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The beginning of snowmelt caused some flooding and county roads to wash away east of Rexburg.  Madison County closed two roads due to wash out and one due to two mud slides near each other. The mud slides occurred on 6000 East. The roads are in largely agricultural areas. Few homes were in the areas and had secondary means of access to their properties. A canal filled with water and the canal side failed. Water from this failure ran past a neighborhood, with little damage to driveways reported.  Road damage was estimated at 100 thousand dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The beginning of snowmelt caused some flooding and county roads to wash away east of Rexburg. Few homes were in the areas and had secondary means of access to their properties. We also had a canal fill with water and the canal side fail. Water from this failure ran past a neighborhood, with little damage to driveways reported.  Road damage was estimated at 100 thousand dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed during the afternoon and evening of May 21, some of them approaching severe strength. An approaching trough helped create lift for storms, and favorable CAPE and shear profiles led to the severe risk. A severe thunderstorm watch was issued for Idaho and Clearwater counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm went through Syringa and the Apgar Campground, northeast of Lowell. A weather spotter reported trees down along US Highway-12 from Apgar Campground to just downstream of Swan Creek. The spotter also mentioned roof damage in Syringa and a power outage from Syringa to the Selway River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow loading collapsed a detached garage in Preston as well as collapsing a roof at the Blue Moon Bar and Grill on South East 1st Street in Lava Hot Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A season of continued heavy snowfall resulted in the roof of the Blue Moon Bar and Grill collapsing on the 1st in Lava Hot Springs.  It occurred around 330 PM MST on South 1st East Street.  Multiple people escaped the building with some injuries occurring.  One woman was transported to Portneuf Medical Center for minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several reports of land spouts and funnel clouds occurred on May 26th, May 27th, and May 31st, 2023 near Star, ID and Twin Falls, ID. The radar signatures for both events were unremarkable, leading to the conclusion that these vortices were likely originating from low level and surface directional wind shear and cumulus congestus clouds on the fringes of larger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo submitted on social media from a trained spotter showed a funnel cloud just northeast of his location.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos on social media showed a land spout near Grandview. Reports state at least 3 of them formed in the same area, lasting around 5 minutes each.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous pacific cold front brought heavy snow and strong winds across Lemhi County.

A period of intense snowfall, with wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph associated with snow squalls, was observed, particularly on Idaho State Highway 28 south of Salmon to Gilmore Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted mountain areas across Lemhi County as a mid-level low pressure system tracked across southern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals include: 6 to 8 inches at Williams Creek Summit and 15 inches in Cobalt.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals include: 8 to 12 inches above 5,000 feet, with 2 inches in Salmon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts from the 24th through the 26th were 10.4 inches at the Island Park COOP site, 12 inches at Macks Inn, and the White Elephant SNOTEL had an estimated 14 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two vehicle collision resulted in the death of one of the passengers.  Idaho State Police investigaed a vehicle fatality collision which occurred on Monday, December 4, 2023, at 7:05 A.M. on State Highway 33 at mile marker 57 east of Terreton.||A 2008 Chevrolet Silverado was traveling eastbound on SH33. A 2018 Volvo semi-truck hauling a trailer was traveling westbound on SH33. The Chevrolet crossed the center line where it struck the Volvo head on.||The driver of the Chevrolet, a 23-year-old-man from Terreton, was not wearing a seatbelt and succumbed to his injures at scene. The driver of the semi-truck, a 24-year-old-man from Louisville, KY, was wearing a seatbelt and was transported to a local hospital.  Slick roads were thought to be a contributing factor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm moved over the Lewiston Idaho area and caused flash flooding. The KLWS ASOS recorded 0.68 inches of rain from 1250LST to 1356LST. Strong winds also knocked down several trees in Latah County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding impacted several streets and avenues of Lewiston. It stalled vehicles and caused debris build up in the area. A local emergency was declared from damages sustained during the event. Half an inch of rain fell in 15 minutes from 1330LST to 1345LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough moved into the Intermountain West, bringing widespread precipitation during the night of January 18th. A cold front passed during the night, changing rain to snow in the Boise metropolitan area which created especially icy and slippery roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports measured 3.0 inches of snow in Star, Eagle, and downtown Boise from the snow storm. The Ada County Sheriff attributed 48 total traffic accidents to the snowfall and resulting slippery road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Consalus fire started on June 30 from lightning and continued through July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Consalus fire started on June 30 by lightning. It continued through the month of July. It has burned 475 acres of timber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage led to localized heavy snows and snow squalls in parts of southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and strong winds brought snow squall conditions from 8 to 9 am in Pocatello and Chubbuck with wind gusts 30 to 40 mph and visibility under one quarter mile in heavy snow.   3.6 inches of snow fell at the Pocatello Regional Airport with 6 to 8 inches in Chubbuck, Fort Hall and Pocatello.  There was a department of Highways report of around 12 inches in Pocatello.  Multiple vehicle rollovers and many accidents were reported along interstate 15 near Fort Hall as well with the heavy snow and winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low brought wraparound precipitation to the region. Warmer surface temperatures allowed for heavy rainfall with resulting ponding on major roads and intersections in the Snake River Valley, while heavy snow accumulated in elevations above 5000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station at BSUI1 measured 1.32 inches of heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved through the area bringing areas of hail and strong winds. The areas impacted included Southern Bonner County and Northern Kootenai County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upslope enhanced snow band set up over the Magic Valley and Eastern Idaho due to a closed low to the southeast, which created heavy snowfall mainly over the Interstate 84 corridor. This snow caused traffic accidents and widespread travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Members of the public reported 9.0 inches seven miles south of Kimberly, 4.5 inches in Kimberly, and 8.0 inches in Filer, ID.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorm activity across North Idaho. It brought several reports of damage, power outages, and large hail. It briefly generated a funnel cloud in the North of Hayden Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two reports of one inch hail from a thunderstorm in the area. The storm also caused ponding on roadways and widespread minor damage. The damage included tree limbs and downed power lines. It also damaged docks and boats along Lake Coeur D'Alene. An estimated 1700 customers lost power. Max wind gusts recorded was 39 MPH in Coeur D'Alene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalous moisture and a vigorous shortwave caused a semi-continuous line of thunderstorms that brought heavy rainfall across north-central Idaho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad ridge of high pressure set-up over the region, allowing dangerous heat over the course of several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad ridge of high pressure set-up over the region, allowing dangerous heat over the course of several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime high temperatures ranged from 105-115 degrees each afternoon. In addition, overnight lows remained in the mid to upper 60s, and even low 70s in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed on interstate 86 just east of Pocatello to the Pocatello airport exit and a 30 vehicle crash occurred with multiple injuries and one death involved.   Visibility was under one quarter mile at the Pocatello airport ASOS at the time and Idaho State Patrol determined fog was a contributing factor.  The interstate was closed east bound at the crash site for over 7 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed on interstate 86 just east of Pocatello to the Pocatello airport exit and a 30 vehicle crash occurred with multiple injuries and one death involved.   Visibility was under one quarter mile at the Pocatello airport ASOS at the time and Idaho State Patrol determined fog was a contributing factor.  The interstate was closed east bound at the crash site for over 7 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to parts of southeast Idaho.  The heaviest snow was in the Eastern Magic Valley where widespread locations received over a foot of snow.  The Shoshone Lava Bed region and Lower Snake Plain also received heavy snow.   It was a rare event where low elevations received much heavier snow than the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of snow measured in Albion with 6 inches 5 miles west southwest of Almo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought high winds, rain and snow to southeastern Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front changed rain to snow and combined with strong winds to bring power outages reported by Idaho Power for over 1,500 people in Power, Bingham and Bannock Counties in the Aberdeen, Crystal and Pocatello areas.  A total of 2 to 5 inches of snow fell in the Pocatello and American Falls region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to parts of southeast Idaho.  The heaviest snow was in the Eastern Magic Valley where widespread locations received over a foot of snow.  The Shoshone Lava Bed region and Lower Snake Plain also received heavy snow.   It was a rare event where low elevations received much heavier snow than the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to 12 inches of snow fell in the Shoshone area with 6 inches in Richfield and 5 inches in Dietrich.   Idaho Power reported power outages in Shoshone, Richfield and Dietrich due to the heavy wet snow and strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to parts of southeast Idaho.  The heaviest snow was in the Eastern Magic Valley where widespread locations received over a foot of snow.  The Shoshone Lava Bed region and Lower Snake Plain also received heavy snow.   It was a rare event where low elevations received much heavier snow than the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell on Thanksgiving with 11.5 inches 4 miles east southeast of American Falls, 8 inches in Aberdeen, and 5 to 7 inches in the Pocatello and Chubbuck area.   Idaho Power due to the heavy snow and wind reported power outages in Aberdeen, Springfield, Moreland, Pingree, and Blackfoot on Thanksgiving night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to parts of southeast Idaho.  The heaviest snow was in the Eastern Magic Valley where widespread locations received over a foot of snow.  The Shoshone Lava Bed region and Lower Snake Plain also received heavy snow.   It was a rare event where low elevations received much heavier snow than the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy snow fell in the Eastern Magic Valley over the Thanksgiving holiday.   Reported amounts were 10 to 13 inches in Burley, 13 inches in Declo, 14 inches in Paul and Rupert, and 16 to 18 inches in Heyburn.   Some damage occurred due to the extremely heavy snow.   A pine tree snapped off in Declo, a tarp and metal gazebo collapsed at a private residence in Burley and large tree limbs also downed in Burley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves tracked across North Idaho Panhandle bringing several days of light to moderate snow fall. Snowfall was heaviest in mountains with amounts ranging from 8 to 12 inches. The low lands received amounts ranging from 3 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer 4 miles southeast of Colburn recorded 13.1 inches from December 1 through 4. an inch and half was recorded on the December 1. 8 inches of snowfall was recorded on December 2. Another 3.3 was recorded December 3. Three tenths was recorded on the December 4. Several trained spotters throughout Bonner and Boundary counties reported varying amounts of 2 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves tracked across North Idaho Panhandle bringing several days of light to moderate snow fall. Snowfall was heaviest in mountains with amounts ranging from 8 to 12 inches. The low lands received amounts ranging from 3 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer 3 miles south of St Maries recorded 9 inches of snow over the period. Half on inch fell on December 1. 6.7 inches was measured on December 2. Another 1.8 inches fell on the 3. Other snow reports near Cataldo and Pinehurst measured 4 and 3.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave with low level jet support pushed through the region on November 11. It brought strong winds across North Idaho Panhandle. The Central Idaho Panhandle received gusts in the range of 35 to 45 MPH. North Idaho and Lower Idaho Panhandle areas received winds in the 20 to 30 MPH. Around 700 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local utility company reported 208 customers without power 3 miles Northeast of Oldtown. A wind gust of 34 MPH was recorded near Oldtown at 1615LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just before 3 pm a pickup truck crashed into a semi tractor trailer and both individuals in the pickup truck died due to the accident.   One individual in the semi was airlifted to a local hospital.  The accident occurred 1 mile south of Macks Inn on US Highway 20.  Small hail accumulating to 3 to 4 inches in a 30 minute time frame was believed to be a significant contributing factor in the accident according to Idaho State Police.   The Highway was shut down for 4 hours due to the accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just before 3 pm a pickup truck crashed into a semi tractor trailer and both individuals in the pickup truck died due to the accident.   One individual in the semi was airlifted to a local hospital with injuries.  The accident occurred 1 mile south of Macks Inn on US Highway 20.  Small hail accumulating to 3 to 4 inches in a 30 minute time frame was believed to be a significant contributing factor in the accident according to Idaho State Police.   The Highway was shut down for 4 hours due to the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves tracked across North Idaho Panhandle bringing several days of light to moderate snow fall. Snowfall was heaviest in mountains with amounts ranging from 8 to 12 inches. The low lands received amounts ranging from 3 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 7 inches of snow 3 miles southwest of Huetter. Other reports in the Coeur D'Alene area ranged from 4 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river with a warm air push brought another round of snow to the region. Mountains received near 12 inches of snow. The lowlands received a range of 4 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 7 inches of snow 2 miles northwest of Newman Lake. An Athol Cocorahs observer reported 6.8 inches. Other reports from Boundary, Bonner, and Northern Kootenai counties ranged from 2 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low passed through the Pacific Northwest and brought the first round of widespread snow to the Idaho Panhandle. Snow amounts ranged from a trace to an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho Transportation Department reported a jack knifed semi blocked both Eastbound and Westbound lanes of Interstate 90 near milepost 73. Light snow had been falling for the day. Lookout Pass snotel recorded near 2 inches of snow for the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave with low level jet support pushed through the region on November 11. It brought strong winds across North Idaho Panhandle. The Central Idaho Panhandle received gusts in the range of 35 to 45 MPH. North Idaho and Lower Idaho Panhandle areas received winds in the 20 to 30 MPH. Around 700 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway sensor near Chatcolet recorded a gust of 39 MPH at 1245LST. Local utility company reported 143 customers without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the region, bringing severe winds to the Magic Valley. This brought brief power outages to areas near Twin Falls, otherwise minor impacts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at the Gooding airport, about 3 miles west of Gooding, ID measured a wind speed of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low brought significant wraparound precipitation and isolated supercells to the region. Extremely heavy rainfall was reported with these sets of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station, RENI1, near Reynolds Creek, measured 0.61 inches of heavy rain 10 miles west of Murphy, ID.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused fire on July 18 in Shoshone County. It continued to be monitored through September 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Sawtooth Fire on July 18. It continued to be monitored through September 21. It covered 800 acres of timber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ridge Creek Wildfire started on August 3 by unknown causes. It continued through September 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ridge Creek Wildfire was started by unknown causes along the North of Trail 234 and on both sides of Hayden Creek 437 Road. It burned 4474 of timber and grass. No structures lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought some severe weather to southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust measured at Sawtooth Portable site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought some severe weather to southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked over a large tree in Ashton onto a pickup truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought some severe weather to southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported downed from thunderstorm winds in Chester.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water ran across Old Highway 191 at several locations between Idaho 36 and Devil Creek Reservoir due to heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph wind gust measured in Jefferson County.
Buckskin 2 Wildfire was started on August 5 by unknown causes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buckskin 2 Wildfire was started by unknown causes near the upper end of Twin Creek and Buckskin Saddle. It burned 242 acres of Timber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Consalus fire started on June 30 from lightning and continued August 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Consalus fire started on June 30 by lightning. It continued until August 30. It has burned 475 acres of timber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused fire on July 18 in Shoshone County. It continued through August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Sawtooth Fire on July 18. It continued to smolder through August. It covered 800 acres of timber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary upper level low over the Four Corners region brought periods of heavy rain and thunderstorms which caused localized urban flooding in south central Idaho. Rainfall totals were around 0.50 inches, but the previous days rainfall made areas more prone to localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported on Highway US-95 from Mile Post 0-35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of off and on snow for the first 4 days of December caused travel problems and widespread heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 11 inches of snow fell at the Hilt's Creek SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow set up across the region with a series of disturbances that caused periods of moderate to heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow occurred, causing widespread snow-covered roads. Difficult travel conditions were reported by the Idaho Transportation Department on Highway 12 near and over Lolo Pass. Elk River picked up over 15 inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow set up across the region with a series of disturbances that caused periods of moderate to heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Difficult travel conditions were reported on Highway 12 at Lolo Pass by the Idaho Transportation Department. Chains were required over the pass for a period of time. Lolo Pass picked up around 17 inches of total snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant plume of moisture moved into the region, producing heavy snow in the higher elevations of north-central Idaho. Light freezing rain also occurred in some of the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow occurred with 10 inches reported in Headquarters and Pierce. A report of 11 inches was received from Elk River. Roads were snow covered across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant plume of moisture moved into the region, producing heavy snow in the higher elevations of north-central Idaho. Light freezing rain also occurred in some of the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of light snow and freezing rain fell, leading to slick roadways. Gusty winds combined with the falling precipitation decreased visibility. The public reported slick road conditions and multiple accidents along Highway 95 between Whitebird Hill and Grangeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage led to localized heavy snows and snow squalls in parts of southeast Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was recorded 3 miles north northeast of Driggs and wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph caused slick roads in Idaho Highway 32 causing a slide off of a large oversized load 2 miles northwest of Judkins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough brought two strong storms in short progression over a two day period in the beginning of December. Strong winds and high snowfall totals made travel difficult in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CoCoRaHS station ID-EL-11 near Mountain Home, 20 miles southeast of Boise measured 2.6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant snowfall of the season fell in the southeast Idaho mountains.  Some high elevation SNOTEL sites measured estimated amounts over a foot with 6 inches in the Island Park area and a few inches at some low elevation sites.  Some accidents reported in Fremont County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Franklin Basin SNOTEL site measured an estimated 12.5 inches of snowfall and the Emigrant Summit site 5.6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to the Clearwater Mountains of north-central Idaho. This brought slick roads in the backcountry for hunters and recreationalists.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Savage Pass SNOTEL picked up 8 inches of snow while Lolo Pass received 9 inches. Mountain Meadows which is located south of Red River Hot Springs picked up 3 inches of snow. Temperatures fell into the 20s from Lolo Pass to Powell on US-12 which could have produced black ice on the 25th.
